summarization.a: This figure illustrates the conversion-fluorination mechanism for thermal ALE of SiO₂. Starting from an AlF₃/Silicate surface (left), TMA exposure converts SiO₂ to Al₂O₃/Silicate while releasing volatile AlFₓ(CH₃)₃₋ₓ and SiFₓ(CH₃)₄₋ₓ products, leaving a CH₃ terminated surface (right). HF exposure then fluorinates the Al₂O₃/Silicate intermediate back to AlF₃/Silicate, releasing H₂O and CH₄. The cyclic process achieves net SiO₂ removal (dotted line indicates etched thickness).

a.question: Why can SiO₂ be etched by the conversion-fluorination method but not by standard fluorination and ligand exchange?
a.answer: Standard fluorination and ligand exchange fails for SiO₂ because the Si-F bond is too strong to undergo effective ligand exchange to generate volatile Si-containing products. In conversion-fluorination, TMA converts SiO₂ to Al₂O₃ (thermodynamically favorable with ΔG = −235 kcal/mol at 300°C), which can then be fluorinated and removed via the standard ligand exchange pathway.

a.question: What volatile products are released during the TMA step versus the HF step?
a.answer: TMA step releases AlFₓ(CH₃)₃₋ₓ and SiFₓ(CH₃)₄₋ₓ; HF step releases H₂O and CH₄.

a.question: What do the red and blue colored surface layers represent?
a.answer: The red layer represents AlF₃/Silicate (after HF fluorination), while the blue layer represents Al₂O₃/Silicate (after TMA conversion).

a.question: How does the SiO₂ etch rate depend on TMA pressure in this process?
a.answer: The etch rate increases with TMA pressure: 0.027 Å/cycle at 0.1 Torr up to 0.31 Å/cycle at 4.0 Torr, indicating a pressure dependent reaction mechanism.
